sentences.text: Well, in our situation-- this is maybe advantage that we had, and-- and basically otherwise I don't know if I would have survived-- is because we came from a different labor camp.
We did not go through any selections.
We just got off the train and put into the camp directly.
We just marched into the camp.
There was not a question of selection, not a question of picking people or-- in any way.
But-- because we were just-- everybody went in there, everybody became numbered and got a place to-- to stay.
I never heard about Auschwitz.
Never heard about any of the situation.
And Blizyn, again, I don't-- I don't think that anybody was aware of it.
You know, you used to hear the stories of the Germans put you to-- to work camp, and you join the work.
And this is what you were hoping for.

And maybe I'll backtrack a little bit, because going back, when the Germans was out there in the beginning, 1939, my dad used to say, well, he lived through World War I, and he was occupied by the Germans, and they were not terrific, but you were able to live.
You were able to survive.
You were able to think.
So it was never in their mind, in-- in any way, what could possibly happen [? to ?] [? you ?].
So we definitely did not-- when we came to Auschwitz, I-- I did not know of any situation, did not have any inkling what is going to happen to me.
And again, it is-- of course, I was a boy of 16 who is supposed to be mature, but you have to understand that war broke out 11.
I-- maturity-- sometimes you grow maturity in one way, but I lived in a limbo.
I didn't go to school.
I-- I just just didn't know.
I didn't think of-- of world situation, just-- just did your survival of day.
Not even thinking how do you survive, just by instinct survive.
So were your brother and you in the same barracks at Auschwitz?
Were you able to stay together?
Yes, in Auschwitz I was.
In Auschwitz.
When they sent us to Birkenau-- from Auschwitz we went to Birkenau-- in Birkenau, I got separated from my brother.
And again, it's a question of luck.
By being 16, they put me in a children's barrack.
Not-- not a [? hold-- ?] just a barricade itself.
There were, I don't know, 13, 14 barracks in that particular section of the camp, and I was in this children's barrack which consisted of all kind of kids.
Some kids from Belgium who even had their hairs-- long hairs and were dressed and in regular clothes, didn't have a uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were-- this particular barrack was also our job.
We had a disinfection.
What we would do is take clothes from the-- the inmates and disinfect them in hot water, because the lice was there.
So this was our work.
We were under jurisdiction of a German criminal who was the kapo in there.
He was in camp because of a criminal, but he was in charge.
He was handicapped.
He was limping on one foot.
I don't remember.
I should know his name because for some reason or other he took a liking to me.
I was kind of his pupil.
And we would-- we had a wagon-- no horse, but a wagon with four wheels.
And we would go-- the next barrack to us was a women barrack, and we would go pick up clothes in the women's barrack.
But his excuse was to go to see some ladies at the women's barrack and for us to bring the clothes back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] never mistreated me.
He [SIGHS] gave me food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, it's a difficult question to say, but I'm gonna talk about it anyway.
He definitely tried take advantages of me, sexual, but never forced himself into me.
He made me masturbate him by hand.
He tried once I should do a fellatio on him, but I refused, and he never forced me into it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Otherwise, he-- he definitely never mistreated in any way, or hurt me or-- or-- in any way possible.
So my life in Birkenau was, for food, [? deal. ?] I never lacked food, so I physically was building up a resistance of food.

The tragedy was that in Birkenau, after two months-- they had, every few months, they had a-- [SIGHS] when they went through the barracks to see who is able and who was not able, and took my brother to the crematorium.
So I lost my brother in Birkenau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There I was watching the smoke going up from the chimneys.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And, again, [SIGHS] life went on and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as I say, I-- when they went in there I did not have hardship in-- in Birkenau.
I-- I was able to get stockings to wear on my shoes, food was enough, and my work consisted of pushing a wagon or throwing clothes into the boiler.
That it was not-- as I say, my-- my resistance and will built up.
Until middle of '44, 1944.
It was about when the Russians were coming close, till the end of sending out Auschwitz, and I believe it was around that time.
The same barrack, with the same children.
[SIGHS] Then we were sent to Flossenburg.
By train.
We-- they closed up Auschwitz because the Russians were coming in, so they kept shipping us out to different areas.
So we went-- went to Flossenburg,
It was, I believe, not a long train ride and I really don't-- again, don't-- for some reason, don't recollect the train ride.
Just coming there became the real hardship over there.
They put me to work on a stone-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever it's called.
You break stones.
You take stones and you break it for roads.
Yeah.
I think there were about 20,000 inmates from-- from different camps.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The food became completely minimum.
You just had hardly any-- any-- any foods to eat.
And I was never aggressive individual.
I-- you know, I was not a conniver.
I was not aggressive in-- how-- somehow I managed to get the necessities to survive.
And I think what happened was that the little fat I had left from Birkenau nourished me through some of the months in Flossenburg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And we were there in Flossenburg-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] now I don't know.
It might have been that we were in Auschwitz longer.
Anyway, we were in Flossenburg till about March or April of 1945.
[SIGHS] I really don't remember.
I really don't remember.
I think it was fall.
It was most likely fall.
I really don't recall it.
The necessary-- it is going back a long time, and it is something that I have really never talked about, my experience.
There was people-- it is amazing, we mingle between mostly Holocaust survivors, and any subjects we talk-- politics to anything-- we end up talking about the Holocaust.
But I never, for whatever reason-- I have four kids and they knew I'm a survivor, and I never told them the story.
So because of me not being repetitious throughout this year, I guess my memory has elapsed in certain things, especially dates and times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, in Flossenburg, we were until about April, middle of April.
And all the time you were in Flossenburg they had you working in this rock quarry breaking these rocks.
That's where I was.
Eight, 10 hours sitting in cold or sun and breaking those-- those rocks.
You know.
Again, I saw people being killed around me.
I saw people being beaten around me.
If I had an angel looking over me or whatever, I personally was not exposed as individually, but the tragedies and atrocities I saw around me being done.
Not really.
I guess of course, I must have talked to people.

There must have been people, but for some reason or other-- maybe because I was so puny, and maybe because I kind of sheltered in [? there ?], I did not seek people and people somehow did not seek me out.
So I cannot specifically say that I had I mean, close friends that I can recall as-- as individuals.
Of course, all the kids that I was in Birkenau somehow disappeared.
You know, when we went to Flossenburg I just-- because I think when they send out from Auschwitz, they send them to different camps.
And I don't know, none went-- just for some reason I-- I came out at Flossenburg.
Well, I believe it was around April that Flossenburg was getting to be liquidated, [SIGHS] and that was winter time.
It would have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] end of-- no, it was April.
It was still winter.
It was April, I guess there was-- must have been still snow, still winter.
They send us-- they put us in wagons, trains.
Trains consisted of open wagons and closed wagons.
I ended up in one of the open wagons.
There are probably about 70, 80 people in a wagon.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The nourishments that you would get would be that they would throw a few loaves of bread over the wagon.
I don't how they put it inside their other ones, but to our wagons they threw it over the wagon.
Again, I sometimes would land a piece of bread.
How I did, I don't know.
Because again, when you have 70 people trying to get a few pieces of bread-- I know it was snowing.
I loved the snow.
At least I wasn't thirsty, I was able to eat snow.
In the morning, you would wake up and you would find that you're sleeping on corpses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What would they do?
They would-- strong people would pick up the corpses and just threw them overboard.

[SIGHS] The following day, all of a sudden we heard planes flying over us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we heard gunfire, machine fire, and realized that it was American or British planes were shooting up the train.
They were able to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? shoot ?] like a-- like a mortars so they couldn't move anymore.
In that particular wagon, there was no casualties, but I understand there were some other casualties in other wagons and some German casualties.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a few hours, or maybe a day, they were able to bring another locomotive in and we moved again.
But it happened the same thing again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After this, I think they gave up, and we were all assembled in the woods, and we started on this march.

I guess some call it a death march, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because you walked, and if you fell down, you were surrounded by Germans on each side, and if you happened to fall or trip, you never got up again.
Yes, I did.
Yes, I did.
Quite a few of them, quite numerous.
I know a statistic-- I guess there is certain statistic that half the people who walk never survive.
Then it started pouring rain.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How to describe misery like this?
It's indescribable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So it's some evening, and I believe it is-- was around April 24, 25.
It was middle of the night.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just-- we were walking through a little village.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just walked into a dark corner and sat down.
I just couldn't do anymore.
There was no logic.
There was no thoughts what's going to happen to you.
That's it, you know, you just-- there came the break in line, I sat down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, I guess it's a question of luck.
A German passed by, a soldier.
He could have shot me.
But I guess there must have been some other people like me who-- who just gave up.
And he took me into a farm-- a barn-- [SIGHS] where there were eight, 10, 20 people.
I didn't know, it was dark at night and I didn't know.
And he put me there without harming me.
I don't-- don't recall it.
I don't know if he asked me something.
He might have asked me, I don't know.
But I don't recall the details, what it is.
All I know, he didn't harm me.
He-- he took me from that spot into this barn.
[SIGHS] The following morning, no Germans.
We were freed by the Americans.
Also Jews.
They were part of this-- of those people walking.
We were-- somebody shouted, the Americans are here!
We opened the barns, and we walked out.
The little town was called Nuremberg.
It is in Bavaria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went into this farmhouse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were French prisoners of war who used to work for the Germans were there, working for the Germans.
There was no German men.
There was just German womans there.
The two ladies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took me in, undressed me, gave me a hot bath, and gave me food.
And of course, the prisoners gave me food and gave me-- and started out drinking wine at that-- at that time, to help them drink wine.
I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 17-- yeah, 17 years old, 17 and a half.
[SIGHS] Then the next day, I walked around in the barn-- in the-- in the-- in that farmhouse, and it was a barn with that farm.
And I heard some noises.
And there were two men hiding there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were not even aware this was a day after liberation, that Americans were liberated over there.
The two men, Jewish.
I'll mention the name and you know who it is.
His name happens to be Morris Pearl.
And the other man's name was Anszul Lazar.
He was Hungarian, Morris Pearl.
Anyway, I told them that you are liberated, you don't have to worry about anything.
I was the big shot already because I had all those cans of American food that the soldiers gave me and said, hey, here you have food, whatever you want.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is how I was freed.
I had no-- I had no place to go.
Ended up being adopted by Morris Pearl, adopted by taking me under the wing.
Morris was 14 years older than I was, so he was a young man.
So was the other gentleman, Anszul.
Then there were two other men who came together.
So we ended up, four grown ups and myself, we took over an apartment from a German who-- and lived in Nuremberg there.
We had food.
There was food.
It was no problem of getting, you know, we get from the food.
And when I was liberated, too, I was-- there was a Polish lieutenant who spoke Polish-- American Polack-- and he kind of talked to me.
He wanted to take me along with him, but I guess they were shipped out right away and [? he ?] couldn't go.
So I ended up with the five man, with Morris Pearl.
We lived there for a few months, and we-- I had some young friends, too, that came along.
And they worked for American soldiers, and they would bring food, and we would cook, and we would eat, and-- and started to get back to-- to a-- a physical life.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After a few months, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Morris Pearl decided that he heard that he had a sister, and he wanted to go look for his sister.
The other men kind of went their own way.
I had no-- again, no place to go.
It was now a question, where do you go?
What do you do?
We started to get a contact already by people from Israel who was trying to recruit us to go to Israel.
And of course, I put my name in.
And-- but he heard that he has a sister in Bergen-Belsen, so we went to Bergen-Belsen.
I went along with him to Bergen-Belsen.
In Bergen-Belsen, he didn't find his sister, but he found a friend I guess he knew before.
Her name is [?
Tamara, ?] and he married her.
And she had-- [?
Tamara ?] had a brother who happened to live in the city called Marburg, [? along ?] near Frankfurt.
[SIGHS] Ended up that we went from Bergen-Belsen-- there was nobody there-- to Marburg.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Around May of 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Morris Pearl had family, and they took him over to the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- stayed then with his brother-in-law, Nate Rosenblatt, and they got married in Marburg.
I was kind of best man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I had written contact with Morris.
Told him that I rode up and I'm going to go to Israel.
I have nothing.
He told me, listen, you have nobody in Israel.
At least I'm here in America.
Why don't you come to America?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there was-- being still a minor, I was able to register as an orphan, and it took me two weeks and I was in the United States.
I came to New York with a bunch of other kids.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, like any other thing, come to New York they ask, where do you want to go?
The idea was to get the people out of New York as much as possible, so they move them.
I said, I don't know, but I have a friend in Los Angeles.
His name is Morris Pearl.
Now, I don't remember what the organization was.
It was an international organization, not a Jewish organization, that sent me.

And I guess they got in contact with Vista Del Mar here, which I was sent to Los Angeles, went to Morris Pearl because he had an apartment, but the next day got in contact from Vista Del Mar by a social worker.
Her name was Anna [?
Schaefer. ?] Still alive, and I still keep contact with her until today.
She's invited to all my affairs.
And basically, they took me under jurisdiction.
I did not live on the premises of Vista Del Mar, but they were under jurisdiction.
They ask me, what do you want to do?
Send you to school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, I had five years of schooling at home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew, and I was being impressed at home, that schooling is important.
You went to cheder, you [INAUDIBLE].
But it was not driven in-- into my mind, from-- from where I come that-- how important knowledge and education is.
But anyway, I went to Belmont High School, which was on Beverly Boulevard, for half a day.
I went to work the other half a day.
I worked in a knitting factory again.
My job was to make big rolls of wool, and I would put it through a steam press that would shrink it.
So I put the roll in one end and took it out the other end.
Very hard work.
I-- Vista Del Mar found a room for me.
How they found a room, Morris Pearl had an aunt here.
The aunt knew a lady named [?
Sarah ?] [?
Rubenstein ?] who was not economically well-off-- had a big house of the other rooms, rented out a room.
And Sam Steinberg rented a room by Mrs.
Rubenstein. ?] Mrs.
Rubenstein ?] had two kids.
One, a daughter named Selma [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] and one, a boy named [?
Maxy ?] who was six years younger than Selma.
Selma ended to be my wife, almost for 47 years now.
